figures.caption: Fig. 1: Per-RPC energy decomposed into initiation (red) and payload (blue) cost as a function of batch size. Percentages indicate the initiation share. At GNN-typical request sizes (tens to hundreds of nodes), initiation accounts for 90–99% of total energy. Payload cost does not dominate until batch sizes exceed ≈1,000 nodes, well above what distributed GNN training produces per mini-batch.
Fig. 4: Total energy (GPU + CPU, all nodes) at B=2000 under congestion. Annotations show GreenDyGNN’s reduction relative to Default DGL.
Fig. 5: Energy overhead from congestion (percentage increase over each method’s own clean baseline) at B=2000. Lower is better.

TABLE I: Comprehensive results under congestion across all datasets and batch sizes. Energy (GPU, CPU, and Total) is the sum across all four nodes over 30 epochs; ET is mean epoch time. Bold marks the best (lowest) per configuration. All energy in kJ.
Fig. 11: Ablation study under congestion. Removing RL adaptation increases energy on all datasets, with the largest impact on Papers100M (8.6%).
Fig. 6: Total energy (GPU + CPU, all nodes) at B=2000 under clean (no congestion) conditions.
Fig. 7: RL agent behavior on OGBN-Papers100M. Top: rebuild window W chosen by GreenDyGNN (adaptive) vs. fixed static baseline. Bottom: per-epoch cache hit rate.

Fig. 2: GreenDyGNN runtime training pipeline (single-worker view). 1⃝ The graph dataset is partitioned and distributed across nodes. 2⃝ A background thread samples mini-batches from the local partition. 3⃝ Sampled computation blocks retrieve node features from the local feature cache. 4⃝ Cache misses trigger batched remote fetches from the distributed KV store, with up to Q batches resolved concurrently across owners. 5⃝ Resolved features are transferred to the GPU for training. 6⃝ The RL controller observes fetch latencies and cache statistics, then selects the next rebuild window W and per-owner cache allocation at each rebuild boundary.

Fig. 8: Simulator validation across a grid of window sizes and congestion delays. Left: prediction error (%), mean 2.8%. Right: predicted (lines) vs. measured (squares) step time for five delay levels.
Fig. 9: Cumulative energy under congestion across all three datasets. GreenDyGNN accumulates less energy than all baselines, with the gap widening during congested epochs.
Fig. 10: Accuracy vs. wall time under congestion across all three datasets. GreenDyGNN converges faster than all baselines.

Fig. 3: RL agent state inputs and action outputs. The agent observes per-owner congestion multipliers, cache hit rates, system load ratios, and previous actions, and selects a joint rebuild window (W , 8 discrete values) and per-owner cache allocation at each rebuild boundary.
